speaker: interviewer
text: Three, in the interview with Edward Polidi. Please continue, the emotions [BACKROUND NOISE] of the people you played for. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know in this period how much [? cautionately ?] I was realizing that I really loved to be involved with-- [BACKROUND NOISE] with music, musical art at the time. I only felt that this for me was presenting some form of communication, some form of being able to touch the people, be able to-- to watch the faces that were communicating, the eyes that were communicating with me, and the time in this way that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was almost feeling happy at the time that I'm able to perform my little thing without many mistakes. Perhaps these experiences later on left in me this very special sensation of performing in front of audiences, [BACKROUND NOISE] and be able to feel the communication, this invisible spirit of emotional communication with your audience-- audiences. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This also eventually gave me the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] powerful lessons of responsibility of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] performance that is without mistakes when you are in front of a big group of people. But more than that, it is desire to be able to communicate with them something that is familiar to them that they like it, and it's telling them something that they not only enjoy, but they react in this so emotional way. Of course, it was quite well before that my really serious studies will resume and begin, studies with music, studies-- studies with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one instrument like violin. This come considerably later in my life, when [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were able already to settle ourselves and leave the-- this small village. And this was one of the-- maybe last contribution of my mother to my destiny later on in my life, later on to buy-- to find and buy some little violin and start my lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was while you were still in Vidin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was when we returned back to Sofia, which was already three years later, three and a half years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we move ahead to there, can you finish telling me any memories you have of being in Vidin? How long were you there, and with whom were you during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Vidin approximately until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in that-- in those quarter the entire time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We remained there practically until the Red Army invaded Bulgaria. And the Germans disappear. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We understood-- there is something that I remember now. During these three years, what happened was one event in which some group of young people escape from the city, via water, river. And I understood that some of them succeed to reach Cyprus. I don't know how this happened, but they later on succeed to reach Palestine at the time. This was-- the oldest daughter of one of them was the oldest daughter of my uncle. I understood that this was also group of-- a bigger group of young Jewish people that some of them that were catched were hanged and killed, but some of them succeed to reach Palestine. And this is how, after later on, is my relative from what I understood, become important political figure there at the time of the first governments of-- illegal government of Israel, before that Israel would become independent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that cousin's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Margarita, the same like my grandmother of my mother's side, Margarita Cohen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How I understood later on, she become a, a hero, her and her husband that she found there between after [? emigres ?] of young people of other countries that went through the same routes, risking their life. Yeah, this happened there. Other events that I recall of importance [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were only this following, I don't know how the-- the news is-- how the world's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] phenomenon, the world events were arriving to there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents have a radio or access to newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Obviously, they had some access to radio. That I think that in this time were perhaps illegal. Because we didn't have a radio at home. We had a radio at home in Sofia when-- but everything was left there. So I think that perhaps some of the-- the Jewish people, local Jewish people that used to live in the city that's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from long periods of time that help also my parents, used to have some radio, and they used to receive this kind of information of the news there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stated that the Jews from Greece were in transit to other parts of Europe. Were the Bulgarian Jews that were in this area ever deported? Were there roundups ever of them forced to leave this area?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Fortunately enough, they saved their lives by pure luck, because of the development-- this fast development of the events that didn't gave them-- the time of the German army, the Germans, to put them in their-- prepare already ferryboat. Because they were prepared. They were known already which group of Jewish people go to which boat there. And they're prepared, so maybe much more details, I don't know exactly what happened. But I remember that there were staying there on the river already boats, also for the Jews that were-- local Jews that were there. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the withdrawing, the velocity of withdrawing of the German army obviously was with this kind of speed that they didn't succeed to realize this transportation. There was one other phenomenon that happened very accidentally, maybe not accidentally. The story-- the story still is very unclear, very dark about this phenomenon. The Bulgarian king that was suppose to put his signature for the final deportation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was called by Hitler, was asked by Hitler to go to arrive to-- for a certain meeting in Berlin. He never came back. Well, he came back as a form of a cadaver. His body already was transported in the plane. They-- some people say that he was poisoned. Somebody-- other people said that he was killed. It was not known exactly what happened. It only was known that Hitler was asking him to do two things. One was to transport the Jews, and the other to put the Bulgarian soldiers on the Eastern Front against the Russians. And obviously the answer of the king, Boris III, in both of these questions were negative. Because he knew that in both of these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] events, he will touch very deeply the soul of Bulgarian people, and will have tremendously big problems. First of all, Bulgarian people, Bulgarian soldiers, were not going to shoot Russian soldiers. Perhaps they would kill or turn their rifles to their own officers. And the second thing was that Bulgarian population, in-- in-- in large, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had basically a feeling of very great sympathies towards the Jewish people. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people of the villages where they were transported had some kind of a very much this kind of Slavic attitudes of mercy toward these people. That they see that they are basically honest people that are trying within their very meager miserable means to live one honest life, never to steal, never to kill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That they're basically a religious people, that they believe in God, and that one of their main activities was especially among a very big elderly population were to read their Bibles and-- and pray to God. And what was-- that they had one culture, in many respect much superior than the culture of these villages where they were transported. And their behave, was one behave of very high morals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they-- and besides that, they had very many remembrances of when this persecution was not existing, when this-- many of these people were leading intellectuals, writers. As a matter of fact, my uncle is the only one Bulgarian writer that received Nobel Prize. The greatest musicians were also of Jewish descendance. As a matter of fact, one of them, Pancho Vladigerov, was the nephew of one of the very great Russian writers, Boris Pasternak, the author of He have some of his work dedicated to his grandfather, Leonid Pasternak, that was actually the father of Boris Pasternak. Presently [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his name is the title name of the-- the National Academy of Music, National Academy of Music now appears the name of Pancho Vladigerov. He was one very, very great talent, and teaching of many, many of the most gifted Bulgarian musicians, one of which was the very famous pianist Alexis Weissenberg, a worldly famous pianist, his pupil of piano. He also was my teacher of theory. I-- much later on, received also from his son, one dedication. He wishes that during my lifetime that I make a recording of all his works that he composed for violin and piano, something that I carried in my heart for many, many years, and hope one day to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were speaking about the liberation, and the Red Army came into Vidin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was actually one very special moment in the history of Bulgarian people. They really received them like people that free them, that bring open one page of their life with new hopes, hopes for a better life. Perhaps this always happened in the human history, when some changes occur. The people of lower classes, many times, accept this with one very positive attitude with hopes that this change will bring something good in their life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, in this situation it was not much different. I don't know how much good the new era was bringing to the life of Bulgarians. But nevertheless, the new era came. This was the era of socialism in Bulgaria. Now later on the history proved that it brought not much better things in their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the liberation day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was the 9th of September, 1944. This is the day that's really definitely the life of the Jewish people was saved, it was known that they will be not deported. And these iron ferryboats were again relocated in their normal place there in the ports. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Very soon after that, we returned back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your family stayed together during this these three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My family, after approximately one year that we arrived to Vidin, separate. My father was taken in hard labor [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? fit-- ?] locations, location of hard labor. And they said-- they stayed separated until the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where he was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was taken somewhere in the uranium mines, where they were working the uranium from the earth. This was places where they used to send, in peaceful time, the people who are condemned to execution, because of the radiations. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, this is what happened at the time. I saw my father after the 9th of September. I don't recall exactly which day it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came back to you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came back and join us, yes, already when we returned back to our old home in Sofia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, the Russians came. And many of the Bulgarian partisan arrived back to the city, those who are people, basically young people, that used to fight Germans in an illegal way, hiding in the mountains, in the forests. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they, first of all, they came to the city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was actually very big emotions and big agitation between the population. They, first of all, they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] succeed to catch several of the fascist [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] officials, high officials, in the city. And organized so-called-- so-called popular tribunals, in which some of them were executed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or shooted, or hanged even. The prisons were freed of all these political prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I think what was happening, again is one of those mystical form of communication, I don't know. How-- when the Jewish people start to communicated and know where they are supposed to go, and which train they are supposed to take. And they started-- I remember that my mother before that even we arrived to our home, she met with some of other cousins of ours. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with the meager clothings that they had were-- I think that there were not too many problems of furniture and things like that; [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] start to open new page of the life of Jewish people, not for very long, however. We arrive back to our home in order to see the ruins of our home, the home in which I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was via train still, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] carrying the few meager luggages. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I only recall that in the very beginning, this was period in which I was seeing my mother only crying, very deeply depressed. Because was a very big problem at the time to organize the foods, and the settling, resettling. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then came one radiance of the sun when my father came back still alive. He was unrecognizable. I was almost not able to recognize him. He was older maybe, with 50, 60 years older than what I knew him. He actually already had become like old man, with very thin, very-- with beards, white beard. When he left, he was-- had very beautiful black hair, strong black hair, he turned to an old man, almost unrecognizable. This happiness that were existing and my family's cheerfulness, everything was completely lost, everything was killed. It was basically silence and sadness. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the spring was coming. And the nature don't care of what has happened with private human destiny, with all the beauty of new flowers, and romance. [SIGHS] The young people, obviously that have the-- the-- very special quality to forget the bad thing, especially when they're very young, and trying to find the interesting and fascinating-- fascinating things in every new situation was what eventually vitalized my parents. And obviously they had decided, or my mother decides obviously to have other child. I don't know. But nevertheless, she become pregnant. And somewhere in the beginning of December, the new baby appear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 19-- 1945. Before that, however, something else happened. I-- obviously my activity didn't stop, or my love and interest for music didn't stop. And my father and my mother started to find all the means for survival, he started to travel, to try to develop some kind of business. And my mother started to try to develop business at home, as a seamstress, as a seamstress. Obviously, she succeeds to make some money. Because exactly with this money she bought not only my first violin, but she was able to afford my first lessons. I never will forget this was when I played in the Philharmonic Society, once with one lady, young lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first teacher, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop at this point, and go on to the next tape.
